metadata.title: Matter Waves
metadata.notes: Film three — the one the photoelectric outro promised. Tangent EARLY (the equation is the thesis). GRAPHIC ~56% accepted (buildup + scale bar = evidence).
metadata.description_blurb: A doctoral thesis so strange the committee asked Einstein whether to accept it. A lab accident in New Jersey that proved it. And the experiment voted the most beautiful in physics: electrons, one at a time, drawing interference stripes no particle should ever draw.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. De Broglie reversed Einstein's arrow: if waves act like particles, then anything with momentum has a wavelength. The whole thesis is one line, wavelength equals h over momentum, so faster or heavier things have shorter waves. A 150-volt electron's wavelength matches atomic spacing, making the idea testable in Davisson and Germer's accidentally crystallized nickel. Tonomura fired electrons one at a time, and single random dots slowly built into sharp interference fringes.

artifactLines: De Broglie reversed Einstein's arrow: if waves act like particles, then anything with momentum has a wavelength.
The whole thesis is one line, wavelength equals h over momentum, so faster or heavier things have shorter waves.
A 150-volt electron's wavelength matches atomic spacing, making the idea testable in Davisson and Germer's accidentally crystallized nickel.
Tonomura fired electrons one at a time, and single random dots slowly built into sharp interference fringes.

beats.narration_text: De Broglie reversing Einstein's arrow, from waves-as-particles to particles-as-waves, is the move I want to trace numerically. I'll take his one line, wavelength equals h over momentum, and check the famous cases. Can you help me (1) compute why a 150-volt electron lands near one tenth of a nanometer, matching atomic spacing, (2) explain why that coincidence made the idea testable by Davisson and Germer, and (3) show how Tonomura's single-electron dots building into fringes proves the wave belongs to each electron alone?

props.command: De Broglie reversing Einstein's arrow, from waves-as-particles to particles-as-waves, is the move I want to trace numerically. I'll take his one line, wavelength equals h over momentum, and check the famous cases. Can you help me (1) compute why a 150-volt electron lands near one tenth of a nanometer, matching atomic spacing, (2) explain why that coincidence made the idea testable by Davisson and Germer, and (3) show how Tonomura's single-electron dots building into fringes proves the wave belongs to each electron alone?
output: one at a time, still makes fringes: Tonomura, 1989

beats.narration_text: In 1924, a doctoral student handed Paris a thesis so strange the committee nearly rejected it. They asked Einstein. Einstein called it brilliant.
beats.tts_normalized_text: In 1924, a doctoral student handed Paris a thesis so strange the committee nearly rejected it. They asked Einstein. Einstein called it brilliant.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of de Broglie (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Louis de Broglie ~1924, young, formal; set shot.focus on his face

beats.narration_text: His name was Louis de Broglie, and his idea takes one sentence: if light waves act like particles, then particles should act like waves.
beats.tts_normalized_text: His name was Louis de Broglie, and his idea takes one sentence: if light waves act like particles, then particles should act like waves.

beats.narration_text: Einstein had shown light carries momentum — waves behaving like particles. De Broglie simply reversed the arrow: anything with momentum has a wavelength.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Einstein had shown light carries momentum — waves behaving like particles. De Broglie simply reversed the arrow: anything with momentum has a wavelength.

viz.data: left: 'light waves -> particles' with a navy check (Einstein, film two); a terracotta hand-drawn arrow flips the relation: 'particles -> waves?'; the question mark lands last

beats.narration_text: The whole thesis is one line: wavelength equals h over momentum. The faster or heavier the thing, the shorter its wave.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The whole thesis is one line: wavelength equals h over momentum. The faster or heavier the thing, the shorter its wave.

tangent.claim: Everything that moves has a wavelength — everything.

glossary.mean: Planck's constant — same h, third film

example.cost: Heavier things: waves too small for any instrument.

tangent.reentry: And a crystal is exactly where it was tested.

beats.narration_text: Lambda, the wavelength of matter itself. P, the momentum — mass times velocity. And h, Planck's constant again — the same tiny number from the last two films.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Lambda, the wavelength of matter itself. P, the momentum — mass times velocity. And h, Planck's constant again — the same tiny number from the last two films.

beats.narration_text: An electron accelerated through 150 volts: wavelength one tenth of a nanometer. Exactly the spacing between atoms in a crystal. That coincidence made it testable.
beats.tts_normalized_text: An electron accelerated through 150 volts: wavelength one tenth of a nanometer. Exactly the spacing between atoms in a crystal. That coincidence made it testable.

viz.note: VERIFY: 1.226/sqrt(150) = 0.100 nm (chapter shortcut); atomic spacing ~1 angstrom

beats.narration_text: In New Jersey, Clinton Davisson and Lester Germer were firing electrons at nickel, recording boring, featureless scatter. Then an accident contaminated their sample.
beats.tts_normalized_text: In New Jersey, Clinton Davisson and Lester Germer were firing electrons at nickel, recording boring, featureless scatter. Then an accident contaminated their sample.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Davisson & Germer (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Davisson & Germer with their vacuum apparatus, Bell Labs, 1920s

beats.narration_text: Cleaning the nickel with heat turned it into a single crystal — orderly rows of atoms. The boring scatter vanished. Sharp peaks appeared at specific angles.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Cleaning the nickel with heat turned it into a single crystal — orderly rows of atoms. The boring scatter vanished. Sharp peaks appeared at specific angles.

viz.data: left: jumbled dots (polycrystal) with a flat smooth scatter curve; heat chip; right: ordered atom grid, the curve redraws with a sharp navy peak

beats.narration_text: Electrons at 54 volts should diffract like a wave of point one six seven nanometers. Run it through Bragg's law: the peak lands where observed. Exactly.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Electrons at 54 volts should diffract like a wave of point one six seven nanometers. Run it through Bragg's law: the peak lands where observed. Exactly.

viz.data: calc card: 54 V -> λ = 0.167 nm -> Bragg (d = 0.091 nm) -> predicted ≈ observed 50°; 'parameter-free' chip
viz.note: VERIFY: raw geometry gives ≈47°, inner-potential correction closes to 50° (chapter) — 'exactly' refers to the corrected prediction, per chapter's own wording

beats.narration_text: Decades later in Japan, Akira Tonomura made it undeniable. He fired electrons through a splitter one at a time — never two in the machine at once.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Decades later in Japan, Akira Tonomura made it undeniable. He fired electrons through a splitter one at a time — never two in the machine at once.

shot.note: AI-generated portrayal of Tonomura (PORTRAIT preset) — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Akira Tonomura at the Hitachi electron microscope, 1980s

beats.narration_text: Each electron lands as a single dot. Ten dots: random. Two hundred: faint clumps. Six thousand: stripes forming. Seventy thousand: perfect interference fringes.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Each electron lands as a single dot. Ten dots: random. Two hundred: faint clumps. Six thousand: stripes forming. Seventy thousand: perfect interference fringes.

viz.data: THE visual: four detector panels labeled 10 / 200 / 6,000 / 70,000 — dots accumulate from random to fringed (fringe-weighted random sampling)
viz.note: Tonomura 1989 counts per chapter; the most important visual in the chapter

beats.narration_text: The electrons never touched each other. Each one's wave explored both paths at once and interfered with itself. The dot is where the wave collapsed.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The electrons never touched each other. Each one's wave explored both paths at once and interfered with itself. The dot is where the wave collapsed.

viz.data: one terracotta dot enters; its path splits into two ghost navy waves around the biprism wire; they recombine into fringes; one dot lands

beats.narration_text: So why don't you diffract through a doorway? Compute it: seventy kilograms walking equals a wavelength of ten to the minus thirty-five meters.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So why don't you diffract through a doorway? Compute it: seventy kilograms walking equals a wavelength of ten to the minus thirty-five meters.

shot.note: AI-generated plate — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO·ai] person mid-stride through a narrow doorway, natural light (NATGEO preset); set shot.focus on the doorway gap

beats.narration_text: That's twenty orders of magnitude smaller than a proton. Quantum mechanics doesn't switch off at human scale — its stripes just get impossibly fine.
beats.tts_normalized_text: That's twenty orders of magnitude smaller than a proton. Quantum mechanics doesn't switch off at human scale — its stripes just get impossibly fine.

viz.data: log scale bar: electron 0.167 nm · buckyball 2.5 pm · proton 10⁻¹⁵ m · you 10⁻³⁵ m; the last tick lands far off to the right with a hand-ring

beats.narration_text: The frontier keeps moving: sixty-atom carbon balls diffracted in 1999. Molecules of two thousand atoms by 2019. The wave nature never stops. We just stop seeing it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The frontier keeps moving: sixty-atom carbon balls diffracted in 1999. Molecules of two thousand atoms by 2019. The wave nature never stops. We just stop seeing it.

shot.note: AI-generated plate — synthetic, disclose in credits

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO+ANNOTATION·ai] C60 buckyball render, molecular soccer-ball structure (NATGEO-adjacent scientific render); '1999: 60 atoms / 2019: 2,000 atoms' chips at assembly

beats.narration_text: Who was this doctoral student? French aristocracy, a history degree, a Nobel Prize five years after the thesis they almost refused. A story of its own.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Who was this doctoral student? French aristocracy, a history degree, a Nobel Prize five years after the thesis they almost refused. A story of its own.

beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] Louis de Broglie portrait, 1920s, direct gaze; name + dates at assembly; bio kicker per SKILL.md

beats.narration_text: Your move. Ask Bear what the wave function actually is. Paste this into Claude.

props.command: De Broglie showed particles have wavelengths. But what is actually waving? Explain the quantum wave function — is it a physical wave, a probability, or something else entirely?

beats.narration_text: Matter Waves
